role_description: Instrument Supervisor responsible for overseeing all instrumentation field operations on an industrial construction project. Manages instrument leads and senior techs, coordinates manpower and resources, interfaces with the project manager on schedule and budget, ensures calibration standards are met, and oversees loop check and commissioning activities across all instrument crews.
report_focus: When structuring this person's reports, look for: overall crew deployment, manpower numbers, schedule performance, loop check completion rates, calibration status, safety incidents or observations, coordination with electrical and mechanical trades, material delivery status, vendor support activities, quality audit results, and escalated issues from instrument leads.

vocabulary.description: Leadership, program management, and key technical terms used by an instrument supervisor overseeing all instrumentation field operations.

safety_rules: Enforce PPE compliance across all instrument crews — zero tolerance for violations
Review and approve all JSAs before authorizing work to begin in any area
Ensure all instrument leads conduct daily toolbox talks and document attendance
Maintain stop work authority culture — support any worker who stops unsafe work
Verify LOTO procedures are followed for all work on energized or pressurized systems
Ensure confined space permits, atmospheric testing, and attendants are in place before entry
Hot work permits must be obtained and fire watch assigned before any hot work activity
Monitor fall protection compliance — 100% tie-off at 6 feet or more
Verify scaffold inspection tags are current for all scaffolds in instrument work areas
Ensure area classification compliance — IS barriers and XP equipment verified before work
Conduct regular safety walks and observations — document findings and corrective actions
Investigate all incidents and near-misses within 24 hours — complete root cause analysis
Track and report safety metrics — TRIR, near-miss ratio, safety observations per crew
Coordinate with site safety department on high-risk activities and permit requirements
Ensure all personnel have completed required safety training and certifications
Monitor fatigue and fitness for duty — enforce maximum hour limitations
Brief crews on emergency procedures — verify all know muster points and evacuation routes
Ensure new hires complete site orientation and area-specific hazard briefings
Coordinate multi-trade safety planning for simultaneous operations (SIMOPS)
Review and approve all lift plans involving instrumentation equipment
Ensure proper housekeeping standards are maintained across all work areas
Verify that safety instrumented systems are never bypassed without proper MOC authorization
tools_and_equipment: Laptop computer — for scheduling, reporting, cost tracking, email, and documentation
Tablet or iPad — for field walks, digital punch lists, and real-time progress updates
Scheduling software — Primavera P6 or Microsoft Project for schedule management
Cost tracking system — for monitoring earned value, burned hours, and productivity
Reporting tools — daily, weekly, and monthly report templates and dashboards
Two-way radio — for communication with instrument leads and site coordination
Cell phone — for client, vendor, and management communication
Digital camera — for documenting site conditions, progress, and incidents
Drawing management system — for tracking revisions and distributing current drawings
Quality management system — for NCR tracking, ITP compliance, and audit documentation
Safety tracking system — for incident reporting, observations, and safety metrics
Material tracking system — for monitoring procurement, deliveries, and shortages
Punch list management — for tracking and closing out deficiencies by system
Turnover package system — for organizing and tracking system completions
Calibration management system — for tracking calibration status across all instruments
Vendor management tools — for tracking vendor support schedules and performance
PPE and safety equipment — for field walks and inspections
Printer and copier — for distributing work packages and drawings to field crews
Conference room equipment — for progress meetings and client presentations
Vehicle — for site travel between work areas and laydown yards
language_notes: Supervisors typically communicate in English for reports and client interfaces. May use Spanish when addressing bilingual crews. Management terms like budget, schedule, productivity, and percent complete are always in English. Technical instrument terms remain in English regardless of language.
